name: For Loop
desc: A for loop repeats code a specific number of times. It has three parts: initialization, condition, and update.

steps.expl: Initialize i to 1. This runs once before the loop starts.
Check condition: is i (1) <= 5? Yes — enter the loop body.
Print the current value of i, which is 1.
i++ increments i from 1 to 2. Then re-check the condition.
Check condition: is i (2) <= 5? Yes — continue looping.
Print the current value of i, which is 2.
Check condition: is i (3) <= 5? Yes — continue.
Check condition: is i (4) <= 5? Yes — continue.
Check condition: is i (5) <= 5? Yes — continue.
Check condition: is i (6) <= 5? No — exit the loop. Done!
Initialize sum to 0. This will accumulate the total.
Start the loop: initialize i to 1.
Print the final result: the sum of 1+2+3+4+5 = 15.
Initialize i to 5. Counting down this time!
After the loop, print "Go!" — countdown complete!

name: While Loop
desc: A while loop keeps running as long as its condition is true. Use it when you don't know exactly how many iterations you need.

steps.expl: Print the final value of n: 192. Exponential growth in action!
Divide by 10 and floor: 4287/10 = 428.7 → 428. Removes last digit!
Is 3 even? No — go to else branch.
The Collatz sequence from 6 reaches 1 in 8 steps. Every starting number eventually reaches 1 — or so we think!

name: If / Else
desc: Conditional statements let your code make decisions. If a condition is true, run one block; otherwise, run another.

steps.expl: Declare grade without a value — it's undefined for now.
Check: score (85) >= 90? No — skip this branch.
Check: score (85) >= 80? Yes! Enter this branch.
grade = "B". Score 85 falls in the B range (80-89).
Skip remaining else-if and else blocks since we found a match.
x is odd. Print "-7 is odd".

examples.name: Leap Year Check
code: console.log(year, isLeap ? "is a leap year" : "is not a leap year");

steps.expl: isLeap = true. Divisible by 4 but not 100 → leap year!
2024 is a leap year! February has 29 days.

name: Arrays
desc: Arrays store multiple values in an ordered list. Access items by index (starting at 0), and use loops to iterate through them.

steps.expl: push("Cherry") adds "Cherry" to the end of the array.
pop() removes the last item and returns it. removed = "Cherry".
Back to 2 fruits. push adds, pop removes — like a stack!
Create array and initialize sum to 0.
Assume the first element (3) is the maximum.
Start loop from i = 1 (we already checked index 0).
Update max to 8. New maximum found!
